problem_description:
There are n 1-indexed robots, each having a position on a line, health, and movement direction.
You are given 0-indexed integer arrays positions, healths, and a string directions (directions[i] is either 'L' for left or 'R' for right). All integers in positions are unique.
All robots start moving on the line simultaneously at the same speed in their given directions. If two robots ever share the same position while moving, they will collide.
If two robots collide, the robot with lower health is removed from the line, and the health of the other robot decreases by one. The surviving robot continues in the same direction it was going. If both robots have the same health, they are both removed from the line.
Your task is to determine the health of the robots that survive the collisions, in the same order that the robots were given, i.e. final health of robot 1 (if survived), final health of robot 2 (if survived), and so on. If there are no survivors, return an empty array.
Return an array containing the health of the remaining robots (in the order they were given in the input), after no further collisions can occur.
Note: The positions may be unsorted.
 
 
Example 1:


Input: positions = [5,4,3,2,1], healths = [2,17,9,15,10], directions = "RRRRR"
Output: [2,17,9,15,10]
Explanation: No collision occurs in this example, since all robots are moving in the same direction. So, the health of the robots in order from the first robot is returned, [2, 17, 9, 15, 10].

Example 2:


Input: positions = [3,5,2,6], healths = [10,10,15,12], directions = "RLRL"
Output: [14]
Explanation: There are 2 collisions in this example. Firstly, robot 1 and robot 2 will collide, and since both have the same health, they will be removed from the line. Next, robot 3 and robot 4 will collide and since robot 4's health is smaller, it gets removed, and robot 3's health becomes 15 - 1 = 14. Only robot 3 remains, so we return [14].

Example 3:


Input: positions = [1,2,5,6], healths = [10,10,11,11], directions = "RLRL"
Output: []
Explanation: Robot 1 and robot 2 will collide and since both have the same health, they are both removed. Robot 3 and 4 will collide and since both have the same health, they are both removed. So, we return an empty array, [].
 
Constraints:

1 <= positions.length == healths.length == directions.length == n <= 105
1 <= positions[i], healths[i] <= 109
directions[i] == 'L' or directions[i] == 'R'
All values in positions are distinct
starter_code:
class Solution:
    def survivedRobotsHealths(self, positions: List[int], healths: List[int], directions: str) -> List[int]:
test:
def check(candidate):
    assert candidate(positions = [2, 4, 6, 8, 10],healths = [30, 25, 20, 15, 10],directions = "LLRRR") == [30, 25, 20, 15, 10]
    assert candidate(positions = [2, 4, 6, 8],healths = [1, 2, 3, 4],directions = "LLLL") == [1, 2, 3, 4]
    assert candidate(positions = [5, 4, 3, 2, 1],healths = [2, 17, 9, 15, 10],directions = "RRRRR") == [2, 17, 9, 15, 10]
    assert candidate(positions = [1, 2, 3, 4],healths = [10, 20, 30, 40],directions = "RLRL") == [19, 39]
    assert candidate(positions = [100, 200, 300],healths = [10, 20, 30],directions = "LRL") == [10, 29]
    assert candidate(positions = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],healths = [5, 5, 5, 5, 5],directions = "RRRLR") == [5, 5, 5]
    assert candidate(positions = [10, 20, 30],healths = [1, 2, 3],directions = "RRR") == [1, 2, 3]
    assert candidate(positions = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6],healths = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6],directions = "RRLLRR") == [1, 4, 5, 6]
    assert candidate(positions = [1, 3, 5, 7, 9],healths = [5, 8, 2, 6, 4],directions = "RRRLL") == [5, 6]
    assert candidate(positions = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],healths = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],directions = "RRRRR") == [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
    assert candidate(positions = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],healths = [10, 10, 10, 10, 10],directions = "RLRLR") == [10]
    assert candidate(positions = [10, 20, 30, 40],healths = [1, 1, 1, 1],directions = "RRRR") == [1, 1, 1, 1]
    assert candidate(positions = [1, 3, 5, 7],healths = [5, 5, 5, 5],directions = "RLRL") == []
    assert candidate(positions = [10, 20, 30],healths = [3, 2, 1],directions = "LLL") == [3, 2, 1]
    assert candidate(positions = [4, 2, 6, 8],healths = [5, 5, 5, 5],directions = "LRLR") == [5, 5]
    assert candidate(positions = [6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1],healths = [6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1],directions = "LLRRLL") == [6, 3, 2, 1]

    assert candidate(positions = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6],healths = [6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1],directions = "RRLLLR") == [6, 2, 1]

query:
You are an expert Python programmer. You will be given a question (problem specification) and will generate a correct Python program that matches the specification and passes all tests.

### Question:
There are n 1-indexed robots, each having a position on a line, health, and movement direction.
You are given 0-indexed integer arrays positions, healths, and a string directions (directions[i] is either 'L' for left or 'R' for right). All integers in positions are unique.
All robots start moving on the line simultaneously at the same speed in their given directions. If two robots ever share the same position while moving, they will collide.
If two robots collide, the robot with lower health is removed from the line, and the health of the other robot decreases by one. The surviving robot continues in the same direction it was going. If both robots have the same health, they are both removed from the line.
Your task is to determine the health of the robots that survive the collisions, in the same order that the robots were given, i.e. final health of robot 1 (if survived), final health of robot 2 (if survived), and so on. If there are no survivors, return an empty array.
Return an array containing the health of the remaining robots (in the order they were given in the input), after no further collisions can occur.
Note: The positions may be unsorted.
 
 
Example 1:


Input: positions = [5,4,3,2,1], healths = [2,17,9,15,10], directions = "RRRRR"
Output: [2,17,9,15,10]
Explanation: No collision occurs in this example, since all robots are moving in the same direction. So, the health of the robots in order from the first robot is returned, [2, 17, 9, 15, 10].

Example 2:


Input: positions = [3,5,2,6], healths = [10,10,15,12], directions = "RLRL"
Output: [14]
Explanation: There are 2 collisions in this example. Firstly, robot 1 and robot 2 will collide, and since both have the same health, they will be removed from the line. Next, robot 3 and robot 4 will collide and since robot 4's health is smaller, it gets removed, and robot 3's health becomes 15 - 1 = 14. Only robot 3 remains, so we return [14].

Example 3:


Input: positions = [1,2,5,6], healths = [10,10,11,11], directions = "RLRL"
Output: []
Explanation: Robot 1 and robot 2 will collide and since both have the same health, they are both removed. Robot 3 and 4 will collide and since both have the same health, they are both removed. So, we return an empty array, [].
 
Constraints:

1 <= positions.length == healths.length == directions.length == n <= 105
1 <= positions[i], healths[i] <= 109
directions[i] == 'L' or directions[i] == 'R'
All values in positions are distinct



### Format: You will use the following starter code to write the solution to the problem and enclose your code within delimiters.
```python
class Solution:
    def survivedRobotsHealths(self, positions: List[int], healths: List[int], directions: str) -> List[int]:
        
```

### Answer: (use the provided format with backticks)
response:
To solve this problem, we need to simulate the movement and collisions of the robots. The key is to handle the collisions correctly and maintain the order of the surviving robots as specified in the input.

Here's a step-by-step approach to solve the problem:

1. **Sort the Robots by Position**: Since the positions are not sorted, we first sort the robots based on their positions. This helps in simulating the collisions in the correct order.

2. **Use a Stack for Collisions**: We use a stack to keep track of the robots moving to the right ('R'). When we encounter a robot moving to the left ('L'), we check for collisions with the robots in the stack.

3. **Simulate Collisions**:
   - For each robot moving to the right ('R'), push it onto the stack.
   - For each robot moving to the left ('L'), check if it collides with the robot at the top of the stack:
     - If the robot at the top of the stack has lower health, pop it from the stack and decrease the health of the current robot by one.
     - If the robot at the top of the stack has the same health, pop it from the stack and discard the current robot.
     - If the robot at the top of the stack has higher health, decrease the health of the robot at the top of the stack by one and discard the current robot.
   - Continue this process until no more collisions occur for the current robot moving to the left.

4. **Collect the Surviving Robots**: After processing all robots, the stack will contain the robots that are moving to the right and have not been destroyed. We also need to collect the robots that were moving to the left and survived.

5. **Sort the Surviving Robots by Original Position**: Finally, we sort the surviving robots by their original positions to maintain the input order.

Here is the implementation of the above approach:

```python
from typing import List

class Solution:
    def survivedRobotsHealths(self, positions: List[int], healths: List[int], directions: str) -> List[int]:
        # Combine positions, healths, and directions into a single list of tuples
        robots = [(pos, health, direction, index) for index, (pos, health, direction) in enumerate(zip(positions, healths, directions))]
        
        # Sort robots by their positions
        robots.sort()
        
        # Stack to keep track of robots moving to the right
        stack = []
        surviving_robots = []
        
        for pos, health, direction, index in robots:
            if direction == 'R':
                # Push the robot moving to the right onto the stack
                stack.append((health, index))
            else:
                # Handle collisions for the robot moving to the left
                while stack and health > 0:
                    top_health, top_index = stack[-1]
                    if health > top_health:
                        stack.pop()
                        health -= 1
                    elif health == top_health:
                        stack.pop()
                        health = 0
                    else:
                        stack[-1] = (top_health - 1, top_index)
                        health = 0
                
                # If the robot moving to the left survived, add it to the surviving robots list
                if health > 0:
                    surviving_robots.append((health, index))
        
        # Add the remaining robots from the stack to the surviving robots list
        surviving_robots.extend(stack)
        
        # Sort the surviving robots by their original index
        surviving_robots.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])
        
        # Extract the healths of the surviving robots
        return [health for health, index in surviving_robots]
```

This solution efficiently handles the collisions and maintains the order of the surviving robots as required. The time complexity is O(n log n) due to the sorting step, and the space complexity is O(n) for storing the stack and the list of surviving robots.
